responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark faces a core triad of tensions: rapid policy uptake, cost discipline, and resilience against platform-lock-in. The five levers combat these by shifting procurement away from single-vendor dependence, building credible fallback authentication, driving EU-aligned standards, galvanizing governance legitimacy, and securing diverse funding pathways. The conservative-to-radical progression ensures escalating commitment to platform-neutrality while managing risk of inertia and incumbents. Together they align resilience framing with standards pressure and credible technical demonstrations, enabling a credible, legally robust, and politically feasible pathway toward platform-neutral, certified fallback access for Danish digital identity, without prematurely displacing MitID.

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Access and Procurement Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Public sector defines platform-neutral access requirements and initiates a risk assessment for alternative authentication paths, reducing reliance on iOS/Android trust signals. Systemic: Vendor-dependency index falls by 25%, and fallback-readiness tests show a 40% improvement in cross-platform continuity drills. Strategic: Denmark gains procurement leverage and policy credibility to scale platform-neutrality across AltID and future digital identity ecosystems.
options: Adopt a platform-neutral baseline that mandates WebAuthn/OpenID Connect-based authenticators and policy-backed procurement clauses.
Pilot a two-track path combining AltID compatibility testing and a formal fallback protocol with vendor-neutral signal attestation.
Launch a decentralized SSI-based stack with verifiable credentials and a government-backed token-based funding model to fund interoperability.
levers.review_lever: Controls Procurement Velocity vs. Platform-Neutral Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term governance and funding stability across EU integration.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Demonstrator Acceleration Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establish two non-production demonstrators—mobile-oriented on privacy-respecting OS and a hardware-token/browser fallback—to evaluate standards-based authentication against a simulated environment. Systemic: Demonstrations cover 90% of defined test scenarios and reduce mean time to recover from simulated outages by 30%. Strategic: Builds credibility to influence procurement standards and AltID-related design language.
options: Launch dual non-production demonstrators with WebAuthn/OpenID Connect on privacy-respecting platforms and a hardware-token fallback to assess cross-platform interoperability.
Secure formal commitments with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operator to validate non-production fallback paths and publish risk-mitigation plans.
Prototype a decentralized SSI stack with verifiable credentials, GrapheneOS-compatible attestations, and a government-backed token model to fund interoperability.
levers.review_lever: Controls Speed vs. Real-World Feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how demonstrators translate into binding procurement actions or interoperable EU standards.

levers.name: Standards-Pressure and EU Alignment Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Engage with EU/NSIS/ETSI bodies to embed platform-neutral fallback requirements into national and EU standards and publish a gap analysis against AltID. Systemic: European interoperability index improves by 20%, and consented fallback criteria become embedded in 2–3 major procurement frameworks within 18 months. Strategic: Denmark secures a seat in EU wallet standardization and reduces vendor lock-in risk across member states.
options: Engage EU standard bodies to codify platform-neutral fallback requirements into national and EU procurement standards.
Collaborate with EU-Digital Identity Wallet Working Groups to harmonize signals and interoperability tests across member states.
Establish a cross-border, open-source identity stack governance initiative funded by a government-backed grant model and token-based incentives.
levers.review_lever: Controls Compliance Burden vs. Interoperability. Weakness: The options may understate regulatory friction and sovereignty concerns that slow EU-wide standard adoption.

levers.name: Coalition-Building and Public-Trust Governance Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establish a formal, publicly visible coalition including privacy advocates, civil society, academics, security experts, and policymakers; publish a governance charter. Systemic: Public-trust index improves by 15%, and coalition breadth grows by 40% across phase transitions, increasing likelihood of procurement approvals. Strategic: Legitimizes platform-neutral push and reduces policy-blocking risk, enabling sustained advocacy for fallback capabilities.
options: Form a formal multi-stakeholder advisory group with privacy advocates and civil society to guide policy and procurement.
Publish a resilience-focused public report with transparent timelines and decision gates to enhance legitimacy.
Establish a citizen-owned governance layer backed by distributed ledger and smart contracts to fund and oversee fallback infrastructure.
levers.review_lever: Controls Trust vs. Expediency. Weakness: The options fail to address how governance bodies will sustain funding and accountability if political attention wanes.

levers.name: Innovative Funding and Economic Resilience Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Reallocate funding across the three phases with milestone-based disbursements and strict go/no-go gates. Systemic: Budget variance remains within ±5% across phases, reducing financial risk while preserving flexibility for necessary experimentation. Strategic: Ensures ongoing political support, maintaining cadence for platform-neutral investments and credible fallback readiness.
options: Reallocate funding across the three phases with milestone-based disbursements and strict go/no-go gates.
Seek EU co-funding and private-sector contributions to finance demonstration work and standardization efforts.
Create a government-backed sovereign identity fund leveraging a token-based incentive model to underwrite platform-neutral infrastructure and open-source contributions.
levers.review_lever: Controls Budget Rigidity vs. Experimental Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to specify credible exit clauses if funding becomes constrained or delayed.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is explicit cross-government governance and funding coordination for platform-neutral fallback pathways at EU scale; Add 'Form a cross-government platform-neutral procurement framework with fallback certification and open-source reference implementations' to Platform-Neutral Access and Procurement Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s digital identity transformation must reduce platform lock-in while preserving service continuity, governance legitimacy, and EU-aligned interoperability. The five levers address governance, interoperability, procurement signals, citizen engagement, and security diversity, providing a spectrum from cautious reforms to bold, tech-enabled experimentation. This mix segments risk, builds credibility, and creates durable pathways for platform-neutral fallback mechanisms that mesh with AltID and future EU wallet initiatives.

levers.name: Resilience-Driven Identity Governance Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Public sector signs a platform-neutral governance charter and initiates continuity drills across identity touchpoints. Systemic: vendor-dependency index falls by 20%, and fallback-readiness drills improve by 35%. Strategic: Denmark gains durable resilience and a governance framework enabling platform-neutral procurement, but at higher administrative overhead; Trade-off: resilience and continuity vs. slower decision cycles and governance burden.
options: Adopt a platform-neutral governance charter with centralized oversight, baseline fallback requirements, and phased procurement controls.
Create a cross-agency resilience cell to manage procurement standards, continuity planning, and independent audits while piloting AltID-compatible fallback tests.
Deploy a blockchain-enabled governance layer with open-source attestation protocols and a token-based incentive system to crowdsource interoperability decisions.
levers.review_lever: Controls Resilience vs. Agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how rapid crisis-response coordination would be maintained under heavier governance overhead.

levers.name: Open Identity Fabric Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Public sector publishes open APIs and baseline standards for cross-vendor authentication signals. Systemic: cross-vendor interoperability index improves by 25%, reducing cross-vendor integration costs by 15%. Strategic: Denmark achieves durable interoperability with reduced lock-in risk, but governance and maintenance of an open fabric introduce ongoing costs; Trade-off: interoperability expansion vs. governance overhead.
options: Publish open APIs and baseline standards for cross-vendor authentication signals to enable plug-and-play interoperability across platforms.
Develop and pilot reference implementations for an open identity fabric with vendor-neutral attestations and AltID compatibility.
Launch a federated DID-based identity fabric with verifiable credentials, edge nodes, and a government-backed token economy to incentivize open-source collaboration.
levers.review_lever: Controls Interoperability vs. Governance Overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider resource constraints for open-source governance and long-term maintenance costs.

levers.name: Regulatory Signals and Procurement Resilience Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Policy adopts flexible procurement frameworks and initiates a regulatory sandbox. Systemic: Time-to-approval for platform-neutral solutions decreases by 30%; supplier competition increases by 15%. Strategic: Denmark gains leverage in EU policy and reduces lock-in risk, but regulatory complexity may slow adoption; Trade-off: agility vs. standardization.
options: Institute strict, EU-aligned procurement clauses that require vendor-neutral fallback evaluation.
Pilot conditional procurement linking continuity criteria to vendor-agnostic fallback readiness, with staged gates.
Institute an EU-wide regulatory sandbox with token-based incentives to develop platform-neutral fallback modules and AI-driven compliance checks.
levers.review_lever: Controls Compliance Burden vs. Interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to address cross-border legal risk and EU procurement politics.

levers.name: Citizen-Centric Engagement and Transparency Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Public trust metrics improve; participation in engagements increases. Systemic: engagement participation rises by 25%, and policy acceptance improves by 15%. Strategic: Strengthens legitimacy of platform-neutral efforts, but elevates governance overhead; Trade-off: public input quality and reach vs. timely decision-making.
options: Publish a public roadmap with clear milestones, decision gates, and quarterly updates to maintain transparency.
Formalize multi-stakeholder consultations with privacy advocates, academics, and civil society to review drafts and standards.
Implement a tokenized, blockchain-backed citizen budget and decision-making platform to govern fallback investments and procurement choices.
levers.review_lever: Controls Public Trust vs. Governance Overhead. Weakness: The options do not specify safeguards against capture by special interests or manipulation of engagement processes.

levers.name: Security Architecture Diversity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Security posture improves; systemic: mean time to detect breaches reduces by 35% and annual security costs rise by 12% due to diversification. Strategic: Increases resilience and reduces lock-in risk, but adds integration complexity and cost; Trade-off: breadth of security controls vs. management overhead.
options: Adopt diverse hardware-backed and software-based authenticators with vendor-neutral attestations.
Pilot cross-signature verification with multiple attestation providers and independent audits across components.
Implement cryptographic vault with post-quantum security, multi-cloud/multi-provider attestation networks, and an orchestration layer.
levers.review_lever: Controls Security Breadth vs. Integration Complexity. Weakness: The options lack explicit budget constraints and the risk of fragmentation across services.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is a coordinated cross-government and EU-scale funding and governance mechanism to sustain platform-neutral fallback work; Add 'Institute an EU-wide regulatory sandbox with token-based incentives to fund platform-neutral fallback modules and open-source contributions' to Reg Signals and Procurement Resilience Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s digital identity transformation must reduce platform lock-in while preserving service continuity, governance legitimacy, and EU-aligned interoperability. The five new levers introduce governance, interoperability, procurement signals, citizen engagement, and security diversity as distinct pathways, providing a spectrum from cautious reforms to bold, tech-enabled experimentation that collectively pressure incumbents and regulators toward platform-neutral fallbacks and resilient procurement.

levers.name: Cross-Platform Identity Ecosystem Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Public sector defines cross-platform authentication interoperability requirements and a formal fallback protocol. Systemic: cross-vendor integration readiness index improves by 25% and procurement costs drop by 12% due to modular contracts. Strategic: Denmark gains durable platform-neutral capability and stronger alignment with EU wallet initiatives.
options: Publish a cross-platform interoperability baseline with WebAuthn/OpenID Connect and require vendor-neutral attestation in procurement.
Develop a two-track pilot applying AltID compatibility testing and vendor-neutral attestations to demonstrate cross-vendor trust anchors.
Create a platform-neutral, decentralized identity fabric leveraging verifiable credentials and a government-backed token economy to fund interoperability.
levers.review_lever: Controls Platform Lock-in vs. Procurement Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term governance and funding stability across EU integration.

levers.name: Procurement-Driven Continuity Assurance Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Procurement rules include continuity-based gating and defined multi-vendor exit options. Systemic: vendor-lock-in index falls by 18%, and fallback-readiness testing success reaches 75%. Strategic: Governance and procurement gain credibility to drive platform-neutral implementations, but administrative overhead increases.
options: Institute continuity-focused procurement clauses that require multi-vendor attestations and fallback testing with defined exit options.
Establish conditional funding gates tied to procurement flexibility and vendor-neutral signal verification.
Launch a government-backed platform-neutral procurement framework coupled with a living contract model using smart legal agreements and ongoing external security reviews.
levers.review_lever: Controls Continuity vs. Agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how rapid crisis-response coordination would be maintained under heavier governance overhead.

levers.name: Interoperability Funding and EU Alignment Financing Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Open funding channels for cross-border tests. Systemic: EU-aligned funding share increases by 30%, enabling multi-country pilots and reducing cross-border project costs by 15%. Strategic: Denmark secures EU alignment and reduces vendor lock-in risk across member states.
options: Pursue national funding with staged milestones and clear cost-sharing rules.
Create a cross-border grant consortium with EU partners and private sector co-funding to support fallback demonstrations.
Launch a European Platform-Neutral Identity Fund financed by a mixed public-private instrument and token-based incentives for open-source contributions.
levers.review_lever: Controls Funding Access vs. Sovereign Autonomy. Weakness: The options may not consider domestic economic constraints and capacity to absorb cross-border pilots.

levers.name: Civil Society Oversight and Accountability Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Public accountability charter and transparency mechanisms are published. Systemic: civil-society participation increases by 30%, and governance overhead increases by 20%. Strategic: Public trust and legitimacy improve, enabling smoother policy adoption despite higher overhead.
options: Publish a public accountability charter and quarterly transparency reports.
Establish a formal multi-stakeholder oversight board with privacy and procurement experts.
Create a citizen-owned digital identity oversight DAO with token incentives and parliamentary auditing powers.
levers.review_lever: Controls Public Trust vs. Governance Burden. Weakness: The options do not specify safeguards against capture by vested interests.

levers.name: Diverse Cryptographic Ecosystem Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Security posture expands with diversified cryptographic controls. Systemic: algorithm agility index improves by 40%, and annual security costs rise by 12% due to diversification. Strategic: Reduces long-term lock-in risk and fosters innovation, but integration complexity increases.
options: Adopt a multi-signer cryptographic framework with vendor-neutral attestations.
Pilot cross-provider attestation services and independent security reviews across components.
Implement post-quantum cryptography with a distributed cryptographic vault and cross-cloud attestations leveraging multiparty computation.
levers.review_lever: Controls Security Breadth vs. Integration Complexity. Weakness: The options lack explicit budget constraints and risk of fragmentation.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts that are specific to this project's domain (e.g., "[Domain]-[Decision Type] Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

2026-Mar-08

Project start ASAP
